facts.fact: Merge intervals algorithm uses a sort-then-sweep strategy where intervals are sorted by start time (O(n log n)), then merged in a single O(n) pass by comparing each interval with only the most recently merged interval
Sliding window technique reduces time complexity from O(n²) for nested-loop substring problems to O(n) by maintaining a dynamic window with two pointers that expand and contract based on conditions
Monotonic stack achieves O(n) time complexity for range queries despite nested loops because each element is pushed and popped from the stack exactly once, not multiple times
Prefix sum preprocessing enables O(n) precomputation followed by O(1) per-query answering for range sum problems, making total complexity O(n + q) for n elements and q queries
Two sum problem demonstrates space-time tradeoff: brute force is O(n²) time and O(1) space, while hash map approach is O(n) time and O(n) space
Overlap detection for sorted intervals uses condition: start₂ ≤ end₁ (if intervals A and B are sorted by start time, they overlap if B's start is less than or equal to A's end)
Interval tree achieves O(log n + k) lookup time for finding all k intervals that overlap with a query interval, compared to O(n log n) storage for segment trees and O(n) storage for interval trees

Longest substring without repeating characters problem uses sliding window with hash map to track last-seen index of each character, enabling O(n) time and O(min(n, m)) space where m is unique character count
Next greater element problem uses monotonic decreasing stack with O(n) time complexity by traversing array right-to-left and popping elements smaller than current before checking stack top
Google Calendar room booking treats each room as a 'Resource' with its own email address; when a room 'accepts' a calendar invitation, it is booked, enabling conflict detection at reservation time
Earliest deadline first scheduling greedy algorithm solves interval scheduling in O(n log n) time by selecting intervals with earliest finishing times first and removing all intersecting intervals
LeetCode 56 (Merge Intervals) is Medium difficulty and has been asked by Meta, Google, and Amazon in coding interviews; it is a canonical greedy algorithm pattern
Fixed-length sliding window calculates initial window metric once, then slides by adding entering element and subtracting exiting element, maintaining O(n) total time

Variable-length sliding window uses two pointers (left/right) that expand and contract based on condition validity; when condition becomes invalid, left pointer increments until condition is valid again
Segment tree optimized for point queries on arrays with O(log n) range query time; interval tree optimized for overlap queries with O(log n + k) lookup for k results

examples.example: Google Calendar meeting room scheduling uses merge intervals to consolidate user busy times and find free slots; a user's calendar with meetings [9-10], [10-11], [11-12] merges to a single 3-hour busy block [9-12]
LeetCode 1229 (Meeting Scheduler) asks for common time slots between two people's calendars by merging their busy intervals and finding gaps; typical company interviews: Meta, Google, Amazon
Genomic data analysis uses interval trees to find all genetic markers that overlap a query region, essential for DNA sequence alignment and variant calling in bioinformatics pipelines
Stripe payment processing uses sliding window rate limiting to track API calls: a window of 'last 60 seconds' slides as time passes, rejecting calls that exceed threshold
Database query optimization uses prefix sums to compute range aggregates: PostgreSQL range query on sales table [Jan-Dec] with Q4 subquery uses prefix sum on monthly chunks for O(1) Q4 result

Longest substring without repeating characters problem: for string 'abcabcbb', sliding window+hash map finds 'abc' in O(n) time by tracking character indices and moving left pointer when duplicate found
Next greater element in array [2,1,2,4,3]: using monotonic decreasing stack, result is [4,2,4,-1,-1] in O(n) time (not O(n²) nested loop approach)
Netflix content scheduling uses interval merging to identify contiguous broadcast time slots where multiple shows can run without conflict

analogies.analogy: Sliding window is like a train car moving along a track—the window (car) has fixed capacity, and as it moves forward, passengers board at the front and exit at the back, with the total load always computed incrementally

Monotonic stack resembles a stack of plates where you can only add/remove from the top; if a new plate is larger than the top plate, the top plate is meaningless for future 'next greater' queries and is removed
Prefix sum is like a cumulative receipt—instead of summing every purchase from scratch, you carry a running total; range sum is just 'total at position B' minus 'total at position A'
Merge intervals is like consolidating adjacent conference rooms into a single large meeting space; if two rooms' booking times overlap, they become one larger room reservation
Two-pointer technique is like two detectives searching a sorted street from opposite ends—if they don't meet in the middle, they have converged on the solution without checking every building

Hash map in sliding window is like a rolling clipboard tracking which employees are in the 'current office zone'—when you move the window, you update the clipboard instantly instead of recounting everyone
Interval tree is like a library catalog indexed by time ranges—asking 'which books were published between 2020-2023?' is O(log n + k) not O(n) because the index skips irrelevant branches

Sorting for merge intervals is like alphabetizing a messy filing cabinet first—once sorted, overlapping files naturally sit next to each other, so you can merge in one pass instead of comparing everything

key_numbers.metric: Merge intervals time complexity after sorting
key_numbers.context: Sorting dominates the linear O(n) merge pass; optimal for general case with no pre-sorted constraint
Single pass through array with two pointers; dramatic improvement from O(n²) nested loops
Each element touches the stack exactly twice, yielding O(n) total operations despite nested loop appearance
One-time cost to build prefix array; amortized across q queries yields O(n + q) total

key_numbers.metric: Prefix sum query time after preprocessing
key_numbers.context: Range sum via prefix[r] - prefix[l-1] requires constant time lookup regardless of range size

key_numbers.metric: Hash map two sum space complexity
key_numbers.context: Stores entire input array as hash table; trades O(n) space for O(n) time vs O(n²) brute force
log n to find first overlap, plus k results returned; more efficient than O(n) scan for small k

key_numbers.value: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case
key_numbers.context: Self-balancing tree operations; worst case when tree becomes skewed
Stores n intervals in balanced tree; more efficient than segment tree O(n log n)
Canonical interview problem asked by Meta, Google, Amazon; requires pattern recognition in ~2 minutes
Greedy algorithm with preprocessing step of sorting by finishing times

key_numbers.metric: Two sum brute force time complexity
key_numbers.context: Checks every pair; improved to O(n) with hash map at cost of O(n) space

key_numbers.metric: Longest substring without repeating space complexity
key_numbers.context: Hash map stores only unique characters seen so far; m = alphabet size, n = string length

key_numbers.metric: Next greater element time complexity improvement
key_numbers.context: Brute force compares each element with all right-side elements; monotonic stack achieves linear by eliminating redundant comparisons

common_misconceptions.misconception: All nested loops are O(n²) time complexity
common_misconceptions.reality: Nested loops with monotonic stacks, sliding windows, or two pointers are O(n) because each element is processed a constant number of times, not n times

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sorting is unnecessary overhead for merge intervals; just compare all pairs
common_misconceptions.reality: Sorting is critical: O(n log n) sorting guarantees overlapping intervals sit adjacent, enabling one O(n) pass instead of O(n²) pairwise comparisons

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sliding window requires knowing exact window size in advance
common_misconceptions.reality: Variable-length sliding window dynamically adjusts window size based on validity conditions; size is not predetermined

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash map always has O(1) lookup; time complexity is always O(1)
common_misconceptions.reality: Hash map has O(1) average case but O(n) worst case on collision; in sliding window, the amortized cost is O(1) per operation across the entire pass

common_misconceptions.misconception: Interval tree and segment tree are the same data structure
common_misconceptions.reality: Interval tree optimizes overlap queries O(log n + k); segment tree optimizes point queries and range sum O(log n). Interval tree uses O(n) space; segment tree uses O(n log n)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sum only works for addition; not applicable to other operations
common_misconceptions.reality: Prefix sum technique applies to any associative operation: product, XOR, bitwise OR, etc.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Monotonic stack is only for 'next greater element' problems
common_misconceptions.reality: Monotonic stack applies to: next greater/smaller, previous greater/smaller, largest rectangle in histogram, trapping rain water, and other range query problems

common_misconceptions.misconception: Two pointer technique is the same as sliding window
common_misconceptions.reality: Two pointers compare values at pointers and move independently (pair problems, sorted arrays); sliding window maintains validity between pointers (subarray/substring problems)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Adjacent intervals like [1,2] and [2,3] should not merge
common_misconceptions.reality: Adjacent intervals with touching boundaries (end of first = start of second) must merge to [1,3] in calendar/booking systems to accurately consolidate time

common_misconceptions.misconception: Pattern recognition for merge intervals is hard; no universal signal
common_misconceptions.reality: Keywords 'overlapping intervals', 'merging', 'consolidate', 'busy time', 'conflicts' are universal signals to apply merge intervals pattern
